Facts.text: Byzantine senate elects army officer Phocas Emperor in a mutiny against the reigning Emperor Maurice (who is then killed along with all his family)
Pope Alexander III returns from exile to Rome
Polish Prince Leszek I the White is assassinated at an assembly of Polish dukes at Gąsawa

St Clemens Flood: Storm surge overwhelms dikes along Flemish and Dutch North Sea coast, killing thousands; city of Ostend moves church inland to higher ground (an effort repeated twice more over the next two centuries)
Water Geuzen under Captain Slope enters Amsterdam
English parliament expels Jesuits
Cardinal Francesco Albani elected Pope Clement XI
Nicholas Rowe's play "Ulysses" premieres in London
People of Frederick County, Maryland, refuse to pay British Stamp tax
Annapolis, Maryland, becomes US capital (until June 1784)
French take Antwerp in liberation of Belgium
Henry Burden patents horseshoe manufacturing machine (Troy, NY)
Independence of the Duke of Schleswig-Holstein from Denmark.
Female Medical Educational Society forms in Boston
Just past midnight, a sharp jolt likely caused by heavy rains causes Lake Merced, California, to drop 30 feet (9m)
US Civil War: Battle of Chattanooga & Orchard Knob, Tennessee begins, ends with Union victory on 25 November
-25] Battle at Ball's Ferry, Georgia (30 casualties)
The Manchester Martyrs are hung at Salford Gaol, Manchester, England for shooting a police officer
Louis Ducos du Hauron patents a process of making color photographs, in Paris, France
Louis Ducos du Hauron patents trichrome color photo process
Clipper Cutty Sark is launched in Dumbarton, Scotland, as one of the last clippers ever built and the only one still surviving
Columbia, Harvard & Princeton form Intercollegiate Football Association
Amsterdam police attack meeting of social-democrats united
Notre Dame loses its 1st football game 8-0 to Michigan
Opera "Trumpeter of Säckingen" 1st American production (NYC)
Debut of 1st jukebox (Palais Royale Saloon, San Francisco)
General elections in Italy won by the Left group headed by Francesco Crispi
King William III of the Netherlands dies without a male heir, so a special law is passed to allow his daughter, Princess Wilhelmina, to inherit the throne
Battle of Lomani Congo: Belgian unit beats Arabs, 1000-3000 killed
Pierre de Coubertin launches plan for modern Olympic Games at the Union des Sociétés Françaises de Sports Athlétiques AGM
American inventor Andrew Jackson Beard invents the "jerry coupler" to connect railroad cars
Portable pencil sharpener patented by American inventor John Lee Love
Battle at Belmont: General Methuen's British brigades engage Boer force during Second Boer War
Colorado Governor James Peabody sends the state militia into the town of Cripple Creek to break up a miners' strike.
Henry Watson Furness, an American physician from Indiana is named US minister to Haiti
7.17" (18.2 cm) of rainfall, Rattlesnake Creek, Idaho (state record)
Wright Brothers form million dollar corporation to manufacture airplanes
Post Hospital at Presidio, San Francisco, renamed Letterman General Hospital
Wilhelm Kienzl's opera "Der Kuhreigen" premieres at the Volksoper in Vienna, Austria-Hungary
Hamilton Alerts suspended by ORFU for refusing to field a full team in a replay of a protested game
Jim Larkin and James Connolly establish the Irish Citizens Army in order to protect strikers
The US Army withdraws from Mexico
On the Tigris River in Mesopotamia, the Battle of Ctesiphon between Allied and Turkish forces enters its second day
German army commander General Von Seeckt bans NSDAP & KPD
NY Giant Hap Moran runs 91 yards for a TD from a scrimmage
Nationally Crisis Committee forms in The Hague
An Anglo-Ethiopian boundary commission in the Ogaden discovers an Italian garrison at Walwal, which lay well within Ethiopian territory. This leads to the Abyssinia Crisis.
1st issue of Life picture magazine created by Henry R. Luce published
Emile Janson becomes Belgian premier
1st edition of illegal "The Truth" publishes
Romania signs Tripartite Pact
German troops conquer Klin, NW of Moscow
3rd & 5th Romanian army corps surrenders
German 4th & 6th Army surrounded at Stalingrad
Japanese bombing of Port Darwin, Australia
Soviet 21st Army recaptures Kalatsj at Don
US Coast Guard Woman's Auxiliary (SPARS) authorized
1st printing of illegal "Warheid"
British Forces Broadcasting Service begins operations
Philadelphia Phillies owner William D. Cox is permanently banned from baseball for having bet on his own team
US forces take control of Tarawa, Gilbert Island & Makin from Japanese
Most US wartime rationing of foods, including meat & butter, ends
French Navy fire in Haiphong, Vietnam, kills 6,000
The Workers Party of South Korea is founded
French government of Schumann forms
Dr Frank G. Back (NYC) patents lens to provide zoom effects
Howard Swanson's "Short Symphony" premieres
KVFD (now KTIN) TV channel 21 in Ft Dodge, IA (NBC) 1st broadcast
WJBF TV channel 6 in Augusta, GA (ABC) begins broadcasting
For the first time, the Dow Jones Industrial Average closes above the peak it reached just before the 1929 crash
Britain transfers the Cocos (Keeling) Islands in the Indian Ocean to Australia
Vladimir Kuts of the Soviet Union runs Olympic record 28:45.6 to win the 10,000m at the Melbourne Olympics; later also wins 5,000m gold
Dodgers outfielder Frank Howard is voted NL Rookie of Year
Tinseltown dedicated its Walk of Fame at Hollywood Blvd & Vine St
Tiros 2, a weather satellite is launched
Debut of "Doctor Who" the long-running British sci-fi series
Horatio Alger Society founded
Beatles release single "I Feel Fine" backed with "She's a Woman"
31st Heisman Trophy Award: Mike Garrett, Southern Cal (RB)
The Beatles film promotional video clips for 5 of their songs at Twickenham Film Studios to provide to television outlets (in lieu of live appearances)
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Chicago White Sox outfielder Tommie Agee is voted AL Rookie of Year
Milwaukee Bucks make their 1st NBA trade, giving Bob Love & Bob Weiss to Chicago Bulls for Flynn Robinson
Arthur Young resigns as Chief Constable of the Royal Ulster Constabulary (RUC)
KNCT TV channel 46 in Belton/Killeen, TX (PBS) begins broadcasting
China People's Republic seated in UN Security Council
Danny Murtaugh, manager of MLB world champions Pittsburgh Pirates, announces his retirement
Arab summit conference adopts open and secret resolutions on the use of the oil weapons; embargo extended to Portugal, Rhodesia, and South Africa
60 Ethiopia government officials executed
Alexis Argüello of Nicaragua knocks out Mexican defending champion Rubén Olivares in the 13th round at the Forum in Inglewood, California, to claim the WBA world featherweight boxing title
Bob Thomas of Chicago Bears kicks 55-yard field goal
Apneist Jacques Mayol is the first man to reach a depth of 100m undersea without breathing equipment.
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
European weather satellite Meteosat 1 launched from Cape Canaveral
4,800 die in series of earthquakes that devastated southern Italy
National Black Independent Party forms
Challenger moves to Vehicle Assembly Building at Kennedy Space Center (Florida), for mating in preparation for STS-6
FCC drops limits on duration & frequency of TV ads
Soyuz T-9 lands
USSR leaves weapon disarmament talks
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
58 die as Egyptian commandos storm hijacked Egyptair jet in Malta
Retired CIA analyst Larry Wu-tai Chin, arrested of spying for China
France performs nuclear test
President Reaganannounces that he is pocket-vetoing a bill designed to further restrict lobbying by former federal employees.
Yankees sign free agent 2nd-baseman Steve Sax to 3-year contract
Pilots Union give up sympathy strike against Eastern Airlines
Xenophon Zolotas is sworn in as non-party Prime Minister of Greece
Test Cricket debut of Saeed Anwar (Pak v WI), scores 0 & 0
Brigham Young University quarterback Ty Detmer finishes NCAA career with record 4,031 yards passed in a season, and 15,031 yards for career
Martín López-Zubero swims world record 200m backstroke (1:56.57)
Sacramento Kings ends NBA's longest road losing streak at 43 games
10,000,000 cellular telephone sold
The Republic of Angola officially joins the World Trade Organization.
Agreement between Cambodian Prime Minister Hun Sen and his rival, Prince Norodom Ranariddh
Convention on Cybercrime is signed in Budapest, Hungary.
World of Warcraft, multiplayer online role-playing video game released, world's most subscribed game
MS Explorer, a cruise liner sinks in the Antarctic Ocean south of Argentina after hitting an ice field near the South Shetland Islands; all 154 passengers and crew were rescued

Economists at the University of Hawaii report that the state entered recession in the previous quarter based on the drop in tourist figures and growing unemployment, with 8,800 jobs expected to be cut in 2009

Geffen Records releases rock band Guns N" Roses sixth, and to date last, studio album, "Chinese Democracy"; 8 years in production, with costs over $13 million, it is the most expensive rock album ever made
Maguindanao massacre occurs in Ampatuan, Maguindanao, Mindanao, Philippines
The Bombardment of Yeonpyeong occurs on Yeonpyeong Island, South Korea. The North Korean artillery attack kills 2 civilians and 2 South Korean marines.
Arab Spring: After 11 months of protests in Yemen, The Yemeni president Ali Abdullah Saleh Signs a deal to transfer power to the vice president, in exchange for legal immunity.
JR Tokai unveil a maglev LO train prototype capable of travelling up to 500km per hour
"The Day of the Doctor" 50th anniversary episode of "Doctor Who" screens on BBC One, 1st episode to feature 12th Doctor Peter Capaldi
A magnitude 6.7 earthquake strikes central Japan
A suicide bomber kills 40 people at a volleyball tournament in eastern Afghanistan
An adviser to the Kenyan president says the slaughter of 28 people on a bus by the Somali militant Islamist group al-Shabab is intended to create a religious war in the country
Mass protests in Mexico by relatives of 43 missing Mexican students, who the authorities say were murdered by a drugs gang
Brazilian footballer Robinho sentenced to nine years in prison for rape in Italy
Papua New Guinea Police storm Manus Island refugee camp, forcibly removing asylum seekers
Alabama police kill the wrong suspect after a gunman shoots 18-year-old man and 12-year-old girl at Riverchase Galleria Mall in Hoovermall
Italian designers Domenico Dolce and Stefano Gabbana apologize for culturally insensitive video and media posts insulting Chinese culture, cancel Shanghai fashion show
US Federal Climate report estimates climate change will reduce economy by 10% by 2100 with $141 billion cost from heat-related deaths, $118 billion from sea level rise
Sumatran rhino officially declared extinct in Malaysia after last known specimen, 25-year-old Iman, dies of cancer in Sabah, Malaysian Borneo
AstraZeneca is the third drugmaker to report an effective vaccine for COVID-19 (62% or 90% effective depending on how it is given) and easy-to make and distribute
China launches Chang’e-5 mission to the Moon from Wenchang space site, Hainan Island, to collect lunar rock and soil samples
At least 45 people are killed when a bus crashes and catches fire, near Sofia, Bulgaria
Egypt reopens its 3000 year old Avenue of the Sphinxes in Luxor with a grand ceremony

NASA launches its DART Mission, to test technology to prevent future impact on earth by hazardous asteroid, by deliberately crashing a spacecraft into an asteroid (in a real life echo of the movie Armageddon)
European Space Agency is the first to announce the inclusion of a disabled person amongst their new class of astronauts - Britain's John McFall
Russia steps up airstrike campaign on Ukraine, with widespread power blacks across the country and Moldova, Kyiv 80% without power or water

Dallas Cowboys defensive back DaRon Bland breaks NFL single-season record for interception returns for a touchdown with his fifth, in 45-10 win over visiting Washington Commanders; previous record of 4 set in 1971 and most recently tied in 1993
Storm Bert brings high winds and localised flooding to the UK and Ireland leaving at least four people dead
Charlemagnearrives in Rome to investigate the alleged crimes of Pope Leo III
Flemish pretender to the English throne Perkin Warbeck hanged for reportedly attempting to escape from Tower of London. Invaded England in 1497, claiming to be the lost son of KingEdward IV
Queen Catherine Howard. 5th wife of KingHenry VIII, stripped of her title and confined in the Syon Abbey in Middlesex, England
KingPhilip IIconfers with Dutch financial experts
"Areopagitica", a pamphlet byJohn Miltondecrying censorship, is published
French mathematician, scientist, and religious philosopherBlaise Pascalexperiences an intense, mystical vision that marks him for life.
"Harold in Italy", symphonic work for orchestra and viola byHector Berlioz, premieres and at the Paris Conservatoire; Chrétien Urhan soloist, with Narcisse Girard conducting
Italian tenorEnrico Carusomakes his US debut at the Metropolitan Opera House, in New York inGiuseppe Verdi's "Rigoletto"
US PresidentWarren G. Hardingsigns Willis Campbell Act (Beer Emergency Bill) forbidding doctors prescribing beer or liquor for medicinal purposes
Cecil B. DeMille's first version of film "The Ten Commandments" premieres in the USA
Germany'sGustav Stresemann's government falls to the SPD
Noël Coward's "This Was a Man" premieres in NYC
Legendary Delta blues musicianRobert Johnson's first recording session with producer Don Law at the Gunter Hotel, San Antonio, Texas
Clifford Odets' play "Golden Boy" premieres on Broadway in NYC
John Steinbeck's play "Of Mice & Men" premieres in NYC
Nazi Governor of PolandHans Frankrequires Jewish Poles above the age of 11 years to wear a blue Star of David
Arnold Schoenberg's "Ode to Napoleon" premieres in NYC
US 7th army under GeneralAlexander Patchliberates Strasbourg
Washington Redskins quarterbackSammy Baughpasses for 6 touchdowns vs. Chicago Cardinals (45-21)
LA Dodgers shortstopMaury Willsis named National League MVP
Following the protocol after Abraham Lincoln's death,JFK's body lies in repose in the East Room of the White House and viewed by officials and heads of state
LBJproclaims Nov 25 a day of national mourning after the assassination ofJFK
A&M and Island Records release British folk-rock singer-songwriterCat Stevens' fourth studio album "Tea for the Tillerman"; featuring singles "Wild World" and "Father and Son"
George Harrisonreleases "My Sweet Lord" single in the US
President Reaganvetoes House Joint Resolution 357 which called for further appropriations for fiscal year 1982
"A Christmas Carol" directed by Clive Donner starringGeorge C. Scottpremieres in the UK
Boston College QBDoug Flutiepasses (472 yards), including game ending 48 yard TD (Hail Mary Pass) to end game & beat Miami 47-45
Limited-over international cricket debut forWasim Akramfor Pakistan, v New Zealand
Test Cricket debuts of AustraliansDavid Boonaged 23 & Bob Holland aged 38 (v WI)
Second WTA Tour Championship of the year; change of tennis schedule;Martina Navratilovawins 5th straight and 8th overall title with 7–6, 6–3, 6–2 win overSteffi Grafin NYC
German tennis starSteffi Grafclaims her first WTA Tour Championship with a 4-6, 6-4, 6-0, 6-4 win overGabriela Sabatiniof Argentina at Madison Square Garden, NYC
Wayne Gretzkyscores his 600th NHL goal

The "Bounty Bowl": Philadelphia Eagles defeat Dallas Cowboys 27-0 at Thanksgiving in an ill-tempered game, after which Cowboys coachJimmy Johnsonaccuses Eagles coach Buddy Ryan of placing bounties on players
Evander HolyfieldTKOs Bert Cooper in 7 for heavyweight boxing title
Freddie Mercury(45) confirms he has AIDS the day before he dies
"Doggystyle" debut album bySnoop Doggy Doggis released (Billboard Album of the Year 1994)
Czech tennis No. 1 Jana Novotná wins WTA Tour Championship at Madison Square Garden, NYC; beatsMary Pierceof France 7–6, 6–2, 6–3
Jessica Simpsonreleases her debut studio album "Sweet Kisses"
Georgian presidentEduard Shevardnadzeresigns following weeks of mass protests over flawed elections
"Fela!", based on music and lyrics by Nigerian singerFela Kuti, opens on Broadway
Susan Boylereleases her debut album “I Dreamed a Dream” (biggest selling album worldwide 2009, 2010 Billboard Album of the Year)
Filipino boxerManny Pacquiaodefeats American Brandon Rios by unanimous decision to win the WBO welterweight title at Cotai Arena, Macau, China
British Mercedes driverLewis Hamiltonwins season-ending Abu Dhabi Grand Prix at the Yas Marina Circuit to clinch his second F1 World Drivers Championship, 67 points ahead of teammate Nico Rosberg
Magnus Carlsendefends his World Chess Championship title after defeating Vishy Anand
Republicans condemn USPresident Obama's use of executive powers to force through immigration reform
Lydia Ko(18) of New Zealand wins women's LPGA Player of the Year and retains the Race to the CME Globe title worth $1 million
PresidentYahya Jammehof Gambia bans "Khatna", the Islamic ritual practice of genital mutilation of young girls

Phil Mickelsonclaims $9m winner-takes-all prize in dramatic matchplay golf duel withTiger Woods, finishing under floodlights in Las Vegas; match goes to 22nd hole before Mickelson sinks 4-foot putt for rich victory
Charli D'Ameliobecomes the first person to hit 100 million followers on TikTok
US President-electJoe Bidenintroduces his new cabinet including Alejandro Mayorkas 1st Latino head of homeland security, Avril Haines 1st female director of national intelligence
Manuscript of early workings ofAlbert Einstein's theory of relativity "one of the most important scientific documents of the 20th century" sells at auction for $13 million
TV series "Wednesday" produced byTim Burton, starringJenna Ortegaand Gwendoline Christie released on Netflix
Conquest of Seville by Christian troops under KingFerdinand III of Castileafter the city capitulates
III Summer Olympic Games close at Washington University in St. Louis Francis Olympic Field
Chinese steward Poon Lim begins 133 days adrift after British ship SS Benlomond is torpedoed by a German U-boat, leaving him as the sole survivor
Otto I, the Great, East Frankish king and Holy Roman Emperor (962-73), born in East Francia (d. 973)
Alfonso X 'the Wise', King of Castile & Leon, (1252-84), born in Toledo, Spain (d. 1284)
Jean de Dunois "Bastard of Orléans", French soldier who fought with Joan of Arc, illegitimate son of the Duke of Orleans, born in France (d. 1468)
William Fitzalan, 9th Earl of Arundel, English nobleman who fought during the War of the Roses, born in England (d. 1487)
Prospero Alpini, Italian botanist and physician (De Plantis Aegypti liber) known for his study of date palms, born in Marostica, Republic of Venice (d. 1617)
Francisco Manuel de Mello, Portuguese writer (Catalan Revolt), born in Lisbon, Portugal (d. 1666)
Jean Mabillon, French palaeographer and monastic scholar, established principles for dating medieval manuscripts, born near Rheims, France (d. 1707)
Anthonie Heinsius, Dutch statesman, Grand Pensionary of Holland (1689-1720), born in Delft, Dutch Republic (d. 1720)
Johann K. Amman, Swiss-Dutch deaf-mute teacher (Surdus Loquens), born in Schaffhausen, Switzerland (d. 1724)
Ernst Johann van Biron, Russian duke of Courland and regent of Ivan VI, born in Kalnciems, Semigallia (d. 1772)
Thomas Birch, British historian and biographer (History of the Royal Society 1756-57, Copies of Letters relating to the March of General Braddock), born in Clerkenwell, England (d. 1766)[1][2]
Pierre Charles Le Monnier, French astronomer, born in Paris, France (d. 1799)
Spranger Barry, Irish Shakespearean actor, born in Skinner's Row, Dublin (d. 1777)
Etienne Joseph Floquet, French opera composer, born in Aix-en-Provence, France (d. 1785)
Edward Rutledge, American founding father, Governor of South Carolina (1798-1800) and signer of the Declaration of Independence, born in Charleston, British America (d. 1800)
Thomas Lord, English cricket batsman (founder of Lord's cricket ground; Middlesex CCC), born in Thirsk, England (d. 1832)
Gracchus Babeuf, French utopian socialist (Tribun du Peuple) and revolutionary, born in Saint-Quentin, France (d. 1797)
Thomas Attwood, English organist and composer, born in London (d. 1838)
Jan Roothaan, 1st Dutch general of the Jesuits, authorized 1838 Maryland Jesuit slave sale (1829-53), born in Amsterdam (d. 1853)
Johan Frederik van Oordt, Dutch theologian (Groninger direction), born in Rotterdam (d. 1852)
Gábor Mátray, Hungarian composer and musicologist, born in Nagykata, Hungary (d. 1875)
Michail Pogodin, Russian historian and author (Povesti), born in Moscow, Russia (d. 1875)
Horatio Phillips Van Cleve, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Princeton, New Jersey (d. 1891)
William Dennison, American politician (US postmaster general, Governor of Ohio), born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 1882)
Benjamin Mayberry Prentiss, American politician and Major General (Union Army), born in Belleville, Virginia (d. 1901)
Josiah Dwight Whitney, American Geologist who led the California Geological Survey (Mount Whitney and Whitney Glacier are named for him), born in Northampton, Massachusetts (d. 1896)
Isaac Todhunter, British mathematician, born in Rye, England (d. 1884)
John Willis Ellis, American politician, Governor of North Carolina during US Civil War, born in Rowan County, North Carolina (d. 1861)
Charles Meryon, British-French etcher who was color blind, born in Paris (d. 1868)
Kaspar von Zumbusch, German-Austria sculptor of public statues in Vienna, born in Herzebrock, Westphalia (d. 1915)
James Lillywhite, English cricket slow bowler (1st England Test captain; 2 Tests, 8 wickets; Sussex CCC) and umpire (6 Tests 1881–99), born in Westhampnett, England (d. 1929)
Josef Sucher, Hungarian composer and conductor (Aus alten Märchen), born in Döbör, Hungary (d. 1908)
Jonas Basanavičius, Lithuanian nationalist and a signatory of the Act of Independence of Lithuania, born in Oszkobole, Congress Poland (d. 1916)
Frank Friday Fletcher, American Naval Admiral (Medal of Order for Battle of Veracruz), born in Oskaloosa, Iowa (d. 1928)
Frank Hearne, English cricket batsman (2 Tests England, 4 South Africa; Kent, Western Province), born in Ealing, England (d. 1949)
Karl Hjalmar Branting, Swedish politician, first Social Democratic Prime Minister of Sweden,  and co-winner of the 1921 Nobel Peace Prize, born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1925)
Konstantin Korovin, leading Russian impressionist painter, born in Moscow, Russia (d. 1939)
Alberto Williams, Argentine pianist and composer (Etrerno Reposo), born in Buenos Aires, Argentina (d. 1952)
Theo van Rysselberghe, Belgian painter (pointillism), born in Ghent, Belgium (d. 1926)
Henry Bourne Joy, American automobile executive (Packard Motor Car Co.), born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 1936)
Howard Taylor, American tennis player (US National C'ships doubles 1879; singles 1884, 86, 87, 88 runner-up), born in New York City (d. 1920)
Valdemar Poulsen, Danish engineer who invented devices for radio broadcasting, born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1942)
Anatoly Lunacharsky, Russian Marxist revolutionary, author and literary critic, born in Poltava, Russian Empire (d. 1933)
Manuel de Falla, Spanish pianist and composer (Atlántida), born in Cádiz, Spain (d. 1946)
André Caplet, French composer and conductor (Conte Fantastic; Le miroir de Jesus), born on a boat sailing between Le Havre (Seine-Maritime) and Honfleur (Calvados) in France (d. 1925)
Ernest King, US fleet admiral (Chief of Naval Operations in WWII), born in Lorain, Ohio (d. 1956)
Holcombe Ward, American tennis player (US Nat C'ship 1904; President USLTA 1937-47), born in New York, NY (d. 1967)
Johan Conrad Kikkert, Dutch painter and critic who promoted modern art in the Netherlands through Modern Art Circle, born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1965)
Herman Baccaert, Flemish philological and writer (Along the water front), born in Mechelen, Belgium (d. 1921)
Jose Clemente Orozco, Mexican mural painter (Epic of Culture in New World), born in Zapotlán el Grande, Mexico (d. 1949)
Henry Moseley, English physicist (relationship between atomic number and charge of the atomic nucleus), born in Weymouth, England (d. 1915)
Paul Malengreau, Belgian organist, composer, and educator (Brussels Conservatory, 1813-53), born in at Trélon, Nord, France (d. 1956)
Harpo Marx [Adolph], American actor and comedian (Marx brothers), born in New York City (d. 1964)
Nana Bryant, American stage and screen actress (Ladies of the Chorus; Harvey; Brewster's Millions), born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 1955)

Harry Sunderland, Australian rugby league administrator (manager 1929–30 Kangaroo tour of GB; Queensland RFL secretary 1913-22; inspired establishment RL in France), born in Gympie, Australia (d. 1964)
El Lissitzky, Russian avant-garde artist (Book design for "Of Two Squares", Proun), born in Pochinok, Russian Empire (d. 1941)[1][2]
Erté [Romain de Tirtoff], Russian-French artist and designer, born in St. Petersburg, Russia (d. 1990)
Rosetta Duncan, American vaudevillian (Duncan Sisters), actress (Topsy and Eva), and songwriter (Remeberin'), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 1959)
Joris Vriamont, Flemish author and music publisher, born in Lincent, Belgium (d. 1961)
Klement Gottwald, Czech communist politician, Premier and President of Czechoslovakia (1946-53), born in Vyškov, Austria-Hungary (d. 1953)
Ruth Etting, American stage, radio, and screen singer ("Shine On Harvest Moon"; "Ten Cents A Dance"), and actress (Roman Scandals; Ziegfeld Follies), born in David City, Nebraska (d. 1978)
Viktor Kosenko, Russian-Ukrainian pianist, composer (Moldavian Poem), and educator, born in Saint Petersburg, Russian Empire (d. 1938)
Karl Gebhardt, Nazi doctor (executed for war crimes for conducting experiments on concentration camp inmates), born in Haag in Oberbayern, Germany (d. 1948)
Nirad C. Chaudhuri, Indian writer (The Autobiography of an Unknown Indian), born in Kishoregunj, East Bengal, British India (d. 1999)
Marcel Dalio [Israel Moshe Blauschild], French actor (Sabrina, Gentlemen Prefer Blondes, Casablanca), born in Paris, France (d. 1983)
Norman Hunter, English children's writer (Professor Branestawm), born in London (d. 1995)
Aaron Bank, American OSS officer and founder of the US Army Special Forces, born in New York City (d. 2004)
Victor Jory, Canadian-American stage and screen actor (A Midsummer Night's Dream; Kings Row; Manhunt), born in Dawson City, Yukon Territory, Canada (d. 1982)
Mervyn Roberts, Welsh composer, born in Abergele, Denbighshire (d. 1990)
Lars Leksell, Swedish physician, professor of neurosurgery, and inventor of radio surgery, born in Fässberg, Sweden (d. 1986)
Leendert Braat, Dutch sculptor and screenwriter (White World), born in Paris, France (d. 1982)
Nelson S. Bond, American sci-fi writer, born in Scranton, Pennsylvania (d. 2006)
Nigel Tranter, Scottish author and historian (River Realm; Robert the Bruce), born in Glasgow, Scotland (d. 2000)
Hal Schumacher, American baseball pitcher (World Series 1933; MLB All-Star 1933, 35; New York Giants), born in Hinkley, New York (d. 1993)
Peter Saunders, English theater producer created The Mousetrap - world's longest play), born in London, England (d. 2003)
George O'Hanlon, American actor (Life of Riley - "Calvin"' Joe McDoakes shorts), voice actor (The Jetsons - "George Jetson"), and screenwriter, born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1989)
Tyree Glenn, American jazz and session trombonist and vibraphone player (Cab Calloway, Duke Ellington, Louis Armstrong), born in Corsicana, Texas (d. 1974)
Emmett Ashford, American baseball umpire (first certified black umpire), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 1980)
Roger Avon, British actor (Doctor Who, Daleks' Invasion Earth 2150 A.D.), born in Jarrow, Tyne and Wear, England (d. 1998)
Wilson Tucker, American sci-fi author (Time Masters), born in Deer Creek, Illinois (d. 2006)
Ellen Drew [Esther Loretta Ray], American actress (Isle of Dead, Christmas in July), born in Kansas City, Missouri (d. 2003)
John Dehner [Forkum], American actor (The Right Stuff, Big Hawaii, Bare Essence), born in Staten Island, New York (d. 1992)
Julio Cesar Mendez Montenegro, Guatemalan politician, President of Guatemala (1966-70), born in Guatemala City (d. 1996)
Michael Gough, English actor (Hammer Horror Films, Search for the Nile), born in Kuala Lumpur, British Malaya (d. 2011)
Hugh L'Etang, British medical writer and editor (The Pathology of Leadership; Ailing Leaders in Power: 1914-1994), born in England (d. 1996)[1][2]
Cláudio Santoro, Brazilian violinist, conductor, and composer, born in Manaus, Brazil (d. 1989)
Peter Strawson, English philosopher (University of Oxford), born in London, England (d. 2006)
Chalmers Wylie, American politician (Rep-R-Ohio, 1967-93), born in Norwich, Ohio (d. 1998)
Herman Nieland, Dutch organist, pianist, composer (Te Deum Laudamus; Blätterlos), and educator, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1993)
Paul Celan [Antschell], German-Romanian poet (Collected Prose), born in Cernăuți, Romania (d. 1970)
Ferdinando "Fred" Buscaglione, Italian singer and actor, born in Turin, Italy (d. 1960)
Jack Marshall, American jazz and session guitarist, film and television composer, arranger, and record producer (Capitol Records), born in El Dorado, Kansas (d. 1973)
Manuel Fraga [Iribarne], Spanish politician, President of Spanish Galicia (1990-2005), born in Vilalba, Galaicia, Spain (d. 2012)
Angelo John Novarese, American record company founder (Hi Tunes), born in Memphis Tennessee (d. 1996)
Billy Haughton, American harness driver and trainer (Hambletonian x 4; Little Brown Jug x 5; Messenger Stakes x 7), born in Gloversville, NY (d. 1986)
Daniel Brewster, American politician (Rep-D-Maryland 1963-69), born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 2007)
Guy [Gaetano] Molinari, American politician (Rep-New York, 1981-1983), born in New York City (d. 2018)
Nadia Gray [Nadia Kujnir-Herescu], Romanian actress (Naked Runner, Maniac, Candide), born in Bucharest, Romania (d. 1994)
Colin Turnbull, British-American anthropologist (The Forest people), born in London, England (d. 1994)
Paul Richards, American actor (Kiss Daddy Goodnight;  Breaking Point), born in Los Angeles California (d. 1974)
